scenarios.attack_description: A user in the enterprise downloads and executes a malicious file disguised as legitimate software. The malware establishes a foothold, scans the internal network for vulnerable hosts using SMB, exploits EternalBlue, and propagates laterally. Infected machines execute a payload that encrypts files and generates high-volume file operations across the network.

key_attack_behaviors.initial_access: User downloads and executes malicious file from external source
key_attack_behaviors.lateral_movement: SMB scanning and exploitation of internal hosts via port 445
key_attack_behaviors.payload_execution: Execution of encryption routines and spawning of processes across hosts

rate._note: computed from median duration and bytes

dport._note: destination ports observed in filtered UNSW data

feature_constraints._notes: To be populated in Step 2 via UNSW extraction and percentile analysis

temporal_architecture._notes: To be populated in Step 2 based on attack phase analysis

scenarios.tier_notes: To be determined in Step 2 by counting UNSW rows after filtering (TIER 1: >=10, TIER 2: 5-9, TIER 3: <5)

scenarios.attack_description: An attacker gains access to an enterprise host and stages sensitive files for exfiltration. Files are aggregated and transferred internally, then exfiltrated to an external server using FTP or SSH-based protocols. The attack includes intermediate staging steps to obscure the origin of the data.

key_attack_behaviors.initial_access: Unauthorized access to enterprise host via exploit or credential compromise
key_attack_behaviors.lateral_movement: Movement between enterprise hosts to locate and stage sensitive data
key_attack_behaviors.payload_execution: Compression or aggregation of files prior to transfer
key_attack_behaviors.data_exfiltration: Outbound FTP (port 21) or SSH/SCP (port 22) transfer to external server

scenarios.attack_description: An attacker exploits a vulnerable web server by sending crafted HTTP requests that trigger the ShellShock vulnerability. This leads to arbitrary command execution via Bash, allowing the attacker to spawn shells, execute system commands, and potentially establish persistence or exfiltrate data.

key_attack_behaviors.initial_access: Malicious HTTP request exploiting ShellShock vulnerability
key_attack_behaviors.lateral_movement: Limited or none; primarily local command execution
key_attack_behaviors.payload_execution: Execution of bash commands triggered by HTTP requests
key_attack_behaviors.data_exfiltration: Optional; may include outbound connections or file access

behavioral_cues: repeated HTTP requests with abnormal payloads

scenarios.attack_description: An attacker installs and executes Netcat on a compromised host to open a backdoor listener on a high-numbered port. This allows persistent remote access and command execution. The attacker may subsequently download additional tools or pivot within the network.

key_attack_behaviors.initial_access: Execution of Netcat binary on compromised host
key_attack_behaviors.lateral_movement: Optional pivoting to enterprise hosts after establishing foothold
key_attack_behaviors.payload_execution: Opening of listening port and remote shell access
key_attack_behaviors.data_exfiltration: Possible secondary activity via established backdoor

scenarios.attack_description: An attacker accesses sensitive system files such as /etc/passwd, compresses them using gzip, and transfers them to a remote machine using SCP over SSH. This represents a stealthy data exfiltration workflow leveraging legitimate administrative tools.

key_attack_behaviors.initial_access: Unauthorized access to enterprise system via backdoor or credentials
key_attack_behaviors.lateral_movement: Minimal; focused on local file access
key_attack_behaviors.payload_execution: Reading and compressing sensitive files (e.g., passwd)
